name: Brazil
tagline: From the thousand nations of the forest and coast to a Portuguese colony built on sugar, gold, and the largest slave economy of the Americas, and on to an empire, a republic, and a modern democracy.
summary: Brazil's history begins with the diverse Indigenous peoples, perhaps two to four million strong, who inhabited the land for millennia before European contact. Claimed for Portugal after Pedro Alvares Cabral's landfall in 1500, it became a vast colony whose wealth rested first on sugar and later on gold, sustained by the transatlantic slave trade that brought more enslaved Africans to Brazil than to any other country. Independence came in 1822 with the Cry of Ipiranga, creating a singular monarchy, the Empire of Brazil, which abolished slavery only in 1888, the last nation in the Americas to do so, and gave way to a republic the following year. The twentieth century moved through the Old Republic, the Vargas era and its Estado Novo dictatorship, a long democratic interlude, and the military dictatorship of 1964 to 1985. The Citizen Constitution of 1988 restored democracy to what is today the largest nation in Latin America.

founding.label: Independence from Portugal
founding.detail: On 7 September 1822, on the banks of the Ipiranga brook near Sao Paulo, the prince regent Dom Pedro proclaimed Brazil's independence from Portugal in the act remembered as the Cry of Ipiranga, with the words Independencia ou Morte (Independence or Death). He was acclaimed Emperor Pedro I, and Portugal recognised the new nation by the Treaty of Rio de Janeiro of 1825. European presence had begun more than three centuries earlier, when Pedro Alvares Cabral made landfall on the Brazilian coast in April 1500 and claimed the territory for the Portuguese crown. The monarchy itself ended on 15 November 1889, when a military coup deposed Emperor Pedro II and proclaimed the Republic.

quickFacts.label: Pre-contact peoples
quickFacts.value: Perhaps 2-4 million Indigenous people in some 1,000 nations

quickFacts.label: Transatlantic slave trade
quickFacts.value: Largest single destination; roughly 4.8-5 million enslaved Africans arrived

quickFacts.label: Abolition of slavery
quickFacts.value: 13 May 1888, the Lei Aurea; last in the Americas

eras.title: Indigenous Peoples Before Contact
eras.standfirst: For thousands of years before any European sail appeared on the horizon, the land that would become Brazil was home to hundreds of distinct nations, from the Tupi and Guarani of the coast and forests to the riverine societies of Amazonia.
body: Human settlement in present-day Brazil reaches back many thousands of years, and by 1500 the territory was inhabited by an immense diversity of peoples. Estimates of the pre-contact population vary widely, commonly ranging from around two million to several million, organised into perhaps a thousand tribes and nations speaking hundreds of languages. Along the Atlantic coast and through much of the interior lived speakers of the Tupi-Guarani languages, including the Tupinamba and Tupiniquim, whose villages, agriculture, and warfare the first Europeans would describe.

These societies were far from uniform. Many coastal and forest peoples were semi-nomadic, combining the cultivation of manioc, maize, and other crops with hunting, fishing, and gathering. In the Amazon basin, archaeological evidence points to larger and more settled populations than once assumed, managing the forest, building earthworks, and enriching the soils that sustained dense communities. Religious life, kinship, and oral tradition bound these nations together across a vast and varied land.

Contact after 1500 would prove catastrophic. Old World diseases to which Indigenous peoples had no immunity, together with enslavement, displacement, and warfare, caused a demographic collapse over the following centuries. The peoples of this era are the deep foundation of Brazilian society, and their descendants remain an enduring presence in the nation's languages, foods, place-names, and living cultures.

events.title: Ancient settlement of the land
events.summary: Hunter-gatherer peoples occupy what is now Brazil over many millennia, leaving rock art and ancient sites across the interior and the northeast.

events.title: Tupi-Guarani expansion
events.summary: Tupi-Guarani-speaking peoples spread across the Atlantic coast and into the interior, developing the farming and village societies later encountered by Europeans.

events.title: Complex Amazonian societies
events.summary: Riverine peoples of Amazonia sustain substantial settled populations, managing forests and soils on a scale long underestimated by outsiders.

events.title: Some thousand nations at contact
events.summary: On the eve of European arrival, perhaps two to four million people in hundreds of distinct nations inhabit the territory of present-day Brazil.

pullquote.text: The land held not one people but a thousand nations, speaking hundreds of tongues.

eras.title: Portuguese Colony: Sugar, Slavery, and Gold
eras.standfirst: Claimed by Portugal in 1500, Brazil became a colonial society built on the export of sugar and then gold, and on the labour of millions of enslaved Africans, the largest forced migration of the Atlantic slave trade.
body: Pedro Alvares Cabral made landfall on the Brazilian coast in April 1500 and claimed the territory for Portugal. Early settlement focused on the dyewood called brazilwood, which gave the land its name, but from the 1530s the colony's economy turned to sugar. Vast plantations and mills, the engenhos, rose above all in Pernambuco and Bahia, making Brazil for a time the world's foremost sugar producer and the centre of colonial wealth, with Salvador serving as the first capital.

This plantation economy rested on slavery. After the decline of Indigenous enslavement, Portugal turned to the forced importation of Africans on an immense scale. Over more than three centuries Brazil received roughly 4.8 to 5 million enslaved people, far more than any other country in the Americas and close to half of all Africans carried across the Atlantic. Enslaved labour built the colony's sugar, gold, and later coffee economies, while communities of those who escaped formed quilombos, of which Palmares became the most renowned.

In the 1690s the discovery of gold, and later diamonds, in the interior region of Minas Gerais set off a vast rush. Explorers known as bandeirantes had pushed Portuguese settlement far beyond the line agreed with Spain, and the gold cycle shifted the colony's centre of gravity southward; in 1763 the capital was moved from Salvador to Rio de Janeiro. The wealth funded fine baroque towns such as Ouro Preto and deepened both the slave system and Portuguese control. When Napoleon invaded Portugal in 1807, the entire royal court fled to Rio, which from 1808 to 1821 became the seat of the Portuguese empire, a transformation that prepared the way for independence.

events.title: Cabral claims Brazil for Portugal
events.summary: Pedro Alvares Cabral makes landfall on the coast near present-day Porto Seguro and claims the land for the Portuguese crown.

events.title: The sugar economy
events.summary: Sugar plantations and mills worked by enslaved labour rise in Pernambuco and Bahia, making Brazil a leading producer and the colony's chief source of wealth.

events.title: Transatlantic slave trade
events.summary: Brazil becomes the largest single destination of the Atlantic slave trade, receiving roughly 4.8-5 million enslaved Africans over more than three centuries.

events.title: Gold rush in Minas Gerais
events.summary: The discovery of gold in the interior sets off a vast rush, founding towns such as Ouro Preto and shifting the colony's centre southward.

events.title: The court moves to Rio
events.summary: Fleeing Napoleon, the Portuguese royal court relocates to Rio de Janeiro, which becomes the seat of the empire and is opened to wider trade.

figures.name: Pedro Alvares Cabral
figures.blurb: Commander of the fleet that reached the Brazilian coast in 1500 while bound for India, claiming the land for Portugal and inaugurating European colonisation of Brazil.

figures.name: Zumbi dos Palmares
figures.blurb: The last leader of Palmares, the great community of people who had escaped slavery in the northeast. Killed in 1695, he endures as a national symbol of resistance to slavery; the date of his death is now Black Consciousness Day.

pullquote.text: More enslaved Africans were brought to Brazil than to any other country in the Americas.

eras.title: Independence and the Empire of Brazil
eras.standfirst: Brazil won its independence in 1822 not as a republic but as a monarchy, the only enduring empire in the Americas, ruled by the House of Braganza until slavery's abolition and a military coup brought the throne down.
body: When the royal court returned to Portugal, the prince regent Dom Pedro remained in Brazil. On 7 September 1822, learning that Lisbon meant to reduce the colony's autonomy and recall him, he proclaimed independence in the Cry of Ipiranga and was acclaimed Emperor Pedro I. Portugal recognised the new state by the Treaty of Rio de Janeiro of 1825. Unlike its Spanish-American neighbours, Brazil emerged from independence as a single constitutional monarchy rather than fragmenting into republics.

Pedro I abdicated in 1831 in favour of his young son, and after a turbulent regency Pedro II began his personal rule in 1840. His long reign of nearly half a century brought relative political stability, the expansion of the coffee economy that became the empire's new engine of wealth, the building of railways, and Brazil's costly victory in the War of the Triple Alliance against Paraguay. Yet the empire's prosperity still rested on enslaved labour even as such systems were abolished elsewhere.

The pressure to end slavery mounted through the century. A series of measures gradually restricted it until, on 13 May 1888, the regent Princess Isabel signed the Lei Aurea, the Golden Law, abolishing slavery without compensation, the last country in the Americas to do so. Abolition alienated the landed elite, and the throne, weakened and without that support, fell the next year: on 15 November 1889 a military movement led by Marshal Deodoro da Fonseca deposed Pedro II and proclaimed the Republic, sending the imperial family into exile.

events.title: Independence and the Empire
events.summary: Dom Pedro proclaims independence at the Cry of Ipiranga and is acclaimed Emperor Pedro I, founding the Empire of Brazil.

events.title: Reign of Pedro II begins
events.summary: Pedro II assumes personal rule, beginning a long reign that brings political stability and the rise of the coffee economy.

events.title: War of the Triple Alliance
events.summary: Brazil, Argentina, and Uruguay defeat Paraguay in the deadliest war in South American history, at enormous human and financial cost.

events.title: Abolition of slavery
events.summary: Princess Isabel signs the Lei Aurea, abolishing slavery without compensation; Brazil is the last country in the Americas to do so.

events.title: Proclamation of the Republic
events.summary: A military coup led by Marshal Deodoro da Fonseca deposes Pedro II and proclaims the Republic, ending the monarchy.

figures.name: Pedro I
figures.blurb: Portuguese prince who proclaimed Brazilian independence in 1822 and reigned as the first emperor until his abdication in 1831, after which he returned to Europe to secure the Portuguese throne for his daughter.

figures.name: Pedro II
figures.blurb: Reigning from 1831, under regency until 1840 and in his own right thereafter, he presided over decades of stability and growth. Respected as a learned and moderate ruler, he was deposed in the 1889 coup and died in exile.

pullquote.text: Independencia ou Morte! Independence or Death!
pullquote.attribution: Words attributed to Dom Pedro, the Cry of Ipiranga, 7 September 1822

eras.title: The Old Republic
eras.standfirst: The first decades of the Republic were dominated by coffee, oligarchy, and the alternation of power between Brazil's wealthiest states, a system whose contradictions ended in the revolution of 1930.
body: The Republic proclaimed in 1889 was organised by the Constitution of 1891 as a federation of strong states. In practice politics came to be governed by the so-called cafe com leite arrangement, in which the powerful coffee-growing state of Sao Paulo and the cattle state of Minas Gerais traded the presidency between them. Local power rested with rural bosses, the coroneis, who delivered controlled votes, while the great mass of Brazilians remained outside political life.

Coffee, grown in the south and southeast, dominated the economy and shaped foreign relations and finance, its export earnings underwriting the state. To replace enslaved labour, Brazil encouraged mass immigration, and millions of Europeans, above all from Italy, Portugal, and Spain, together with Japanese and Middle Eastern migrants, arrived in the late nineteenth and early twentieth centuries, transforming the cities and the countryside of the south.

Beneath the oligarchic order ran deep tensions: rural revolts and millenarian movements such as Canudos, the rebellion of young army officers known as the tenentes in the 1920s, and growing urban and middle-class discontent. The system finally broke when a disputed presidential election, amid the shock of the Great Depression and collapsing coffee prices, triggered the Revolution of 1930, which swept Getulio Vargas to power and ended the Old Republic.

events.title: First republican constitution
events.summary: The Constitution of 1891 establishes a federal republic with strong states, modelled in part on the United States.

events.title: War of Canudos
events.summary: The army crushes a large millenarian community in the Bahian backlands, exposing the gulf between the republican coast and the impoverished interior.

events.title: Mass immigration
events.summary: Millions of immigrants, especially Italians, Portuguese, Spaniards, and Japanese, arrive to work coffee and settle the south, reshaping Brazilian society.

events.title: Revolution of 1930
events.summary: A disputed election amid the Depression brings down the Old Republic, and Getulio Vargas takes power, beginning a new era.

figures.name: Deodoro da Fonseca
figures.blurb: Army marshal who led the 1889 coup against the monarchy and became the Republic's first head of state, resigning in 1891 after a political crisis.

pullquote.text: Coffee and milk: the presidency passed between Sao Paulo and Minas Gerais.
pullquote.attribution: On the politics of the Old Republic

eras.title: The Vargas Era and Military Dictatorship
eras.standfirst: Half a century shaped by two authoritarian experiences: the rule of Getulio Vargas, including the Estado Novo dictatorship, and the military regime of 1964 to 1985, a period of forced development, censorship, and state repression.
body: Getulio Vargas dominated Brazilian politics for a generation. Brought to power by the Revolution of 1930, he centralised the state, expanded labour and social legislation, and promoted industrialisation. In 1937 he imposed an outright dictatorship, the Estado Novo, ruling by decree, banning political parties, and censoring the press until he was forced from office in 1945. He returned, this time as an elected president, in 1951, but mounting political crisis ended in his suicide in 1954, an event that shook the nation.

The democratic period that followed saw the building of the new capital, Brasilia, inaugurated in 1960 as a bold statement of modernist ambition. But political and economic instability, and Cold War fears of the left, culminated in a coup on 1 April 1964 in which the armed forces seized power. The military would govern Brazil for twenty-one years through a succession of general-presidents.

The dictatorship combined rapid, state-led economic growth, the so-called Brazilian miracle, with severe repression. This hardened dramatically with Institutional Act Number Five of 13 December 1968, which suspended habeas corpus and other civil rights and ushered in the regime's most violent years. The state systematically used censorship, arbitrary arrest, torture, and the killing and forced disappearance of opponents; a later national truth commission documented hundreds of deaths and disappearances and widespread torture. A gradual opening, the abertura, led to an amnesty in 1979 and, in 1985, the return of civilian rule.

events.title: The Estado Novo
events.summary: Vargas imposes a centralised dictatorship, ruling by decree, banning parties, and censoring the press until his ouster in 1945.

events.title: Inauguration of Brasilia
events.summary: The purpose-built modernist capital, designed by Lucio Costa and Oscar Niemeyer, is inaugurated in the interior, shifting Brazil's centre of gravity inland.

events.title: Military coup
events.summary: The armed forces overthrow President Joao Goulart and seize power, beginning twenty-one years of military dictatorship.

events.title: Institutional Act Number Five
events.summary: AI-5 suspends habeas corpus and civil liberties, opening the regime's most repressive phase of censorship, torture, and disappearances.

events.title: Return to civilian rule
events.summary: After a gradual political opening, the military hands power back to civilians, ending the dictatorship.

figures.name: Getulio Vargas
figures.blurb: The dominant figure of twentieth-century Brazilian politics. He led the 1930 revolution, ruled as dictator under the Estado Novo, championed labour rights and industry, returned by election in 1951, and took his own life in office in 1954.

figures.name: Oscar Niemeyer
figures.blurb: The master of Brazilian modernist architecture who designed the principal public buildings of the new capital, Brasilia, giving form to a national project of the mid-twentieth century.

pullquote.text: I leave life to enter history.
pullquote.attribution: Getulio Vargas, from his final letter, 1954

eras.title: Modern Democratic Brazil
eras.standfirst: Since 1985 Brazil has been a democracy under the Citizen Constitution of 1988, navigating hyperinflation and stabilisation, the rise of mass social programmes, corruption scandals, and sharp political polarisation as the largest nation in Latin America.
body: Civilian rule returned in 1985, and in 1988 a constituent assembly promulgated a new constitution, known as the Citizen Constitution for its broad guarantees of civil, political, and social rights. Direct presidential elections resumed in 1989. The young democracy was soon tested: the first directly elected president, Fernando Collor de Mello, was driven from office by impeachment in 1992 amid corruption charges, an early demonstration that the new institutions could hold power to account.

Economic stabilisation came with the Plano Real of 1994, which tamed years of hyperinflation and was associated with President Fernando Henrique Cardoso. From 2003 the governments of Luiz Inacio Lula da Silva expanded social programmes such as Bolsa Familia, and a commodities boom lifted millions out of poverty. Brazil's growing international weight was reflected in its place among the BRICS nations and its hosting of the 2014 World Cup and the 2016 Rio Olympic Games.

The following decade brought turbulence: the sweeping Lava Jato (Car Wash) corruption investigations, the impeachment of President Dilma Rousseff in 2016, the imprisonment and later legal vindication of Lula, and the deeply polarising presidency of Jair Bolsonaro from 2019. After Lula's narrow victory in 2022, supporters of the defeated president stormed the seats of government in Brasilia on 8 January 2023, an assault on the institutions that Brazil's democracy ultimately withstood. Across these decades the country has continued to grapple with inequality, violence, and the global stakes of protecting the Amazon.

events.title: The Citizen Constitution
events.summary: A new constitution restores full democracy and enshrines an expansive charter of civil, political, and social rights.

events.title: Plano Real ends hyperinflation
events.summary: A new currency and stabilisation plan tame runaway inflation, reshaping the economy and politics of the 1990s.

events.title: Lula and social inclusion
events.summary: Luiz Inacio Lula da Silva's governments expand social programmes during a commodities boom, lifting millions out of poverty.

events.title: Impeachment amid Lava Jato
events.summary: President Dilma Rousseff is impeached as the sweeping Car Wash corruption investigation reshapes Brazilian politics.

events.title: Brasilia attacked, democracy holds
events.summary: Supporters of the defeated president storm the Congress, presidential palace, and Supreme Court; the institutions withstand the assault.

figures.name: Luiz Inacio Lula da Silva
figures.blurb: Former metalworker and union leader who rose to the presidency in 2003, expanded social programmes, was imprisoned and later cleared in the Lava Jato affair, and returned to office after the 2022 election.

figures.name: Fernando Henrique Cardoso
figures.blurb: Sociologist and statesman associated with the Plano Real that conquered hyperinflation, and president from 1995 to 2002 during a period of stabilisation and reform.

pullquote.text: Known as the Citizen Constitution, the 1988 charter marked the rebirth of democracy.

figures.name: Pedro I
figures.blurb: Proclaimed Brazilian independence at the Cry of Ipiranga in 1822 and reigned as the empire's first emperor until 1831.

figures.name: Getulio Vargas
figures.blurb: The defining political figure of twentieth-century Brazil, leader of the 1930 revolution and the Estado Novo dictatorship, later an elected president who died by suicide in office.

sources.label: Indigenous Peoples (Indigenas)
sources.publisher: IBGE, Brazilian Institute of Geography and Statistics
